content_en:
The Hijra (Migration) to Medina in 622 CE marks the pivotal turning point of Islamic history and the beginning of the Islamic calendar. Al-Mubarakpuri gives this event the extended treatment it deserves, recognizing that the Hijra was not merely a tactical relocation but a providential turning point that transformed Islam from a persecuted minority movement in one city into a nascent state with the capacity to reshape the world.

The groundwork for the Hijra was laid through a series of meetings during the annual pilgrimage seasons, in which the Prophet ﷺ met with delegations from the city of Yathrib (later renamed al-Madinah al-Munawwarah — the Illuminated City, or Medina). The first formal pledge was the Pledge of Aqabah I (the First Pledge of Aqabah), in which twelve men from the Khazraj tribe of Yathrib pledged to worship Allah alone, abstain from theft, fornication, infanticide, and slander, and to obey the Prophet ﷺ in all that was right. The second, larger gathering — the Second Pledge of Aqabah — involved seventy-three men and two women from both the Aws and Khazraj tribes, who pledged not only personal obedience but military defense of the Prophet ﷺ as they would defend their own wives and children.

With this pledge in hand, the Prophet ﷺ permitted his Companions to begin emigrating to Medina in small groups. Many did so, abandoning homes, businesses, and family ties — in some cases leaving behind property that was immediately seized by their pagan neighbors. This sacrifice was acknowledged by the Quran in verses of extraordinary power: 'Those who emigrated for the sake of Allah after they were wronged — We will surely settle them in this world in a good place; but the reward of the Hereafter is greater, if only they knew' (Quran 16:41).

The Prophet's ﷺ own departure was deliberately delayed. Abu Bakr had prepared two camels and waited for months for the command to leave. When the Quraysh — having learned that the Muslim community was evacuating and understanding what it meant for their ability to suppress Islam — convened a council and decided to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ by having one man from each tribe strike simultaneously so that no single tribe could be held responsible, Allah revealed the plan to the Prophet ﷺ.

On the night of the planned assassination, the Prophet ﷺ asked his young cousin Ali ibn Abi Talib to sleep in his bed wrapped in his green cloak, to create the impression he was still at home — an act of great personal courage on Ali's part. The Prophet ﷺ then went to Abu Bakr's house, and the two departed southward before turning north toward Medina, hiding for three days in the Cave of Thawr while the Qurayshi search parties scoured the region. The miraculous preservation in the cave — a spider's web that appeared to have formed over the entrance, and a dove that nested there, convincing the searchers no one could have entered — is well documented in the seerah literature.

The Prophet ﷺ arrived at Quba, on the outskirts of Medina, on Monday the 8th of Rabi' al-Awwal, in the year that Islamic tradition numbers as the first of the Hijri calendar. He spent four days in Quba and established what Islamic tradition considers the first mosque in Islam — the Mosque of Quba, whose Friday prayer the Quran explicitly honors (Quran 62:9). He then proceeded into Medina proper, where the entire city turned out to welcome him — an arrival so momentous that the women of Medina sang: 'The full moon has risen over us, from the valley of Wada' — words that have echoed in Islamic tradition for fourteen centuries.
